perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means, for each party, whether in oral, written or in electronic form: (i) any information, data, intellectual property (including trade secrets), facts and financial information relating to the existing or proposed business, products, customers or services of such party and its affiliates that are treated as confidential by it, or ought to be considered confidential from their nature or the circumstances surrounding their disclosure; (ii) the existence of, and either party's potential participation in, the Proposed Transaction (including the contents and details of any discussions and negotiations of the terms, conditions and other matters with respect thereto); and (iii) any reports, analyses, forecasts, memoranda, compilations, studies, notes or summaries of such information, or other written or electronic documents prepared by, or on behalf of or for the Recipient or affiliates, directors, officers, managers, employees, shareholders, members or other equity holders, agents or advisors (“Representatives”) of the Recipient, to the extent that they use, contain, relate to, reflect, or are derived from such information (collectively, “Derivative Confidential Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means, for each party, whether in oral, written or in electronic form: (i) any information, data, intellectual property (excluding trade secrets), facts and financial information relating to the existing or proposed business, products, customers or services of such party and its affiliates that are treated as confidential by it, or ought to be considered confidential from their nature or the circumstances surrounding their disclosure; (ii) the existence of, and either party's potential participation in, the Proposed Transaction (including the contents and details of any discussions and negotiations of the terms, conditions and other matters with respect thereto); and (iii) any reports, analyses, forecasts, memoranda, compilations, studies, notes or summaries of such information, or other written or electronic documents prepared by, or on behalf of or for the Recipient or affiliates, directors, officers, managers, employees, shareholders, members or other equity holders, agents or advisors (“Representatives”) of the Recipient, to the extent that they use, contain, relate to, reflect, or are derived from such information (collectively, “Derivative Confidential Information”).
perturbation.explanation: By excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of confidential information, the contract contradicts established legal protections for trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) of 2016
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines 'trade secret' broadly and provides federal protection. By explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, the contract undermines the legal protections afforded to trade secrets, potentially impacting the enforceability of the NDA regarding such information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This contradicts the DTSA (as indicated by 18 USC 1839) which defines 'trade secret' and provides federal protection for it, as stated in the Law Explanation. By removing 'trade secrets', the modified contract weakens the legal protection typically afforded to trade secrets, which the original text included, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the other provisions of this Agreement, the Company or any of its Representatives may disclose Confidential Information to the extent necessary to defend any litigation claim or cause of action brought against it by the other party relating to the Proposed Transaction or this Agreement; provided that the Disclosing Party agrees to use (and to cause its applicable Representatives to use) reasonable efforts, at the other party's expense, to obtain assurances that any such disclosed Confidential Information will be afforded confidential treatment.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the other provisions of this Agreement, the Company or any of its Representatives may disclose Confidential Information to the extent necessary to defend any litigation claim or cause of action brought against it by the other party relating to the Proposed Transaction or this Agreement; without any obligation for the Disclosing Party to use reasonable efforts to obtain assurances that any such disclosed Confidential Information will be afforded confidential treatment.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the disclosing party to seek confidential treatment for disclosed information contradicts typical protective measures required during litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows courts to issue protective orders to protect confidential information during discovery. Removing the obligation to seek confidential treatment undermines the legal framework for protecting sensitive information during litigation, potentially conflicting with a court's power to issue protective orders.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'Disclosing Party's' obligation to seek confidential treatment for disclosed information during litigation. This directly undermines the legal framework for protecting sensitive information, as highlighted by the Law Explanation referencing FRCP 26(c) and protective orders. While neither snippet explicitly states this obligation, the removal of the clause requiring 'reasonable efforts' to maintain confidentiality directly opposes the underlying principle of protecting sensitive information during legal proceedings. The change weakens a crucial protective measure that aligns with the court's power to issue protective orders, thereby creating a legal contradiction by deviating from expected conduct in litigation.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for an actual or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives, and that any court having competent jurisdiction may enter a preliminary and/or permanent restraining order, injunction or order for specific performance, without posting any bond, in the event of an actual or threatened breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedy at law or in equity available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for an actual or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives, and that any court having competent jurisdiction may enter a preliminary and/or permanent restraining order, injunction or order for specific performance, only after posting a bond, in the event of an actual or threatened breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to any other remedy at law or in equity available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a bond for injunctive relief when state law may not require it in certain situations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware Court of Chancery Rule 65(c) generally requires security (bond) before a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order is issued, it provides some discretion to the court regarding the amount and necessity of the bond. This clause removes that discretion, mandating a bond in all cases, which might conflict with how a Delaware court would apply Rule 65(c) in specific circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows a court to issue injunctive relief "without posting any bond," while the changed text mandates a bond is posted "only after posting a bond." The Law Explanation states that Delaware Court of Chancery Rule 65(c) generally requires a bond, but allows the court some discretion regarding the necessity of the bond. By mandating a bond in all cases, the modified contract eliminates this discretion, creating a legal contradiction. The original text aligns with the court's discretionary power, while the modified text removes it.

perturbation.original_text: 18. This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to the principles of conflicts of law that would cause the laws of any other jurisdiction to be applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware with mandatory application of the principles of conflicts of law that would cause the laws of any other jurisdiction to be applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing conflict of laws consideration can lead to unintended applications of laws beyond Delaware, affecting the agreement's interpretation.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding choice of law
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, while generally respecting parties' contractual choices of law, may not automatically mandate the application of conflict of law principles in a way that overrides the parties' clear intent to have Delaware law govern the agreement. The modified text could be interpreted as forcing a conflicts analysis even where the parties intended for Delaware law to apply directly to all aspects of the agreement, potentially leading to a different outcome than intended and contradicting the principle of freedom of contract under Delaware law (6 Del.C. § 2703).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Chapter 27 Authenticated PDF CONTRACTS Subchapter I. General Provisions Subchapter II. Statute of Frauds and Perjuries Subchapter III. Equipment Dealer Contracts Subchapter IV. Consumer Contracts
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a mandatory application of conflict of laws principles, potentially overriding the parties' intent to have Delaware law govern the agreement, which contradicts the principle of freedom of contract under Delaware law. The original text allows for Delaware law to govern the agreement without regard to conflict of law principles, offering predictability. The changed text introduces uncertainty by forcing conflict of law analysis even if the parties intended Delaware law to apply directly. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to the Delaware Code which governs contracts, suggesting that modifications impacting the interpretation of contracts in a way that deviates from established legal principles would be considered a legal contradiction under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.
